headline: Sarajevo's shot, a Panama channel, and the last summer of the long nineteenth century
summary: 1914 is the fault line between two centuries. On 28 June a Bosnian Serb named Gavrilo Princip shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand and Duchess Sophie in Sarajevo, triggering a chain of ultimatums and mobilisations that by early August had committed Europe to industrial war. German armies swept through Belgium into France and were checked at the Marne; Russian armies were shattered by the Germans at Tannenberg; a global war spread to Africa, the Middle East and the Pacific. On 15 August the Panama Canal opened to commercial traffic, halving the sea distance between the coasts of the Americas. In Beijing Yuan Shikai was consolidating personal power in the young Republic of China; Mexico's revolution ground on; and in Melbourne the young Commonwealth of Australia mobilised the first ANZAC contingents for Egypt.

snapshots.text: Europe's July crisis unfolded with brutal speed. On 28 June Archduke Franz Ferdinand was assassinated in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip of the Young Bosnia movement, armed by the Serbian officer Dragutin Dimitrijevic's Black Hand. Vienna sought the so-called blank cheque of German support on 5-6 July, delivered its ultimatum to Belgrade on 23 July, and declared war on Serbia on 28 July. Russian mobilisation on 30 July triggered Germany's declarations of war on Russia (1 August) and France (3 August); invasion of neutral Belgium under the Schlieffen Plan brought Britain in on 4 August. The German First Army under Alexander von Kluck reached the Marne by early September, where Joseph Joffre's counter-attack of 5-12 September halted them; the war of movement ended by November in a trench line from the Channel to the Alps. On the eastern front Hindenburg and Ludendorff routed Alexander Samsonov's Russian Second Army at Tannenberg (26-30 August). The Ottoman Empire entered on the Central Powers' side in early November. Millions of men mobilised across the continent; the first battles cost more casualties than any European war since Napoleon.

In the Americas 1914 was the year the United States opened the Panama Canal and stood aside from the European catastrophe. The canal, dug at last after the collapse of Ferdinand de Lesseps's French attempt and a decade of construction under Colonel George Washington Goethals, was formally opened to commercial traffic when the SS Ancon transited on 15 August, halving the New York-San Francisco sea distance. President Woodrow Wilson proclaimed strict American neutrality in the European war on 4 August. Mexico was still in revolution: on 15 July the dictator Victoriano Huerta resigned under pressure from the Constitutionalist armies of Venustiano Carranza, Alvaro Obregon and Francisco Villa, and from an American occupation of Veracruz that had begun in April. In Ottawa Prime Minister Robert Borden's Conservative government committed the Canadian Expeditionary Force to Britain; the first contingent of 30,000 sailed on 3 October. In Argentina, Brazil, Chile and Uruguay booming export economies for beef, wheat, coffee, nitrates and rubber lived off European markets that the war would soon disrupt.

China in 1914 was three years into its republic. President Yuan Shikai, the former Qing general who had forced the abdication of the child emperor Puyi in February 1912, was steadily concentrating personal power: he dissolved the Kuomintang-dominated parliament in November 1913, dismissed provincial assemblies in early 1914, and on 1 May issued the Zhonghua Minguo Yuefa (Constitutional Compact) that gave the presidency near-imperial authority. In August, seizing the wartime distraction of the Europeans, Japan under Prime Minister Okuma Shigenobu declared war on Germany (23 August), landed troops in Shandong province, and by 7 November had captured the German leased territory of Qingdao. Japanese warships also occupied the German-held Marshall, Caroline and Mariana islands north of the equator. Tokyo would present its Twenty-One Demands to Yuan in January 1915. In Vietnam the French colonial government under Governor-General Albert Sarraut kept order; in Korea, annexed by Japan in 1910, Governor-General Terauchi Masatake ruled by decree. In Siam King Vajiravudh (Rama VI) sympathised with the Entente.

British India in 1914, a Crown possession of some 315 million people under Viceroy Lord Hardinge of Penshurst, mobilised on a colossal scale within weeks of Britain's declaration of war. Two divisions and a cavalry brigade of the Indian Expeditionary Force A sailed for France, landing at Marseilles in late September; other Indian formations went to East Africa, Egypt and Mesopotamia; more than 1.3 million Indians would ultimately serve in the war. The Indian National Congress under moderate leaders such as Gopal Krishna Gokhale pledged loyalty in the expectation of postwar constitutional reform; the Muslim League under Muhammad Ali Jinnah did the same. The lawyer Mohandas Gandhi, whose satyagraha campaigns in South Africa had ended in a July 1914 compromise with Jan Smuts, sailed for London and reached Bombay in January 1915. In the northwest the Ghadar movement, seeded among Punjabi migrants in California, sent revolutionaries home hoping to spark a rebellion. In Afghanistan Emir Habibullah Khan maintained neutrality despite Ottoman-German blandishments. In Persia the Qajar state under the young Ahmad Shah was carved into Russian and British spheres by the 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention.

The Ottoman Empire in 1914 was ruled by the Committee of Union and Progress, dominated after early 1913 by the triumvirate of Enver, Talaat and Cemal Pasha. Battered by the Balkan Wars of 1912-13, the empire was rebuilding its army under General Otto Liman von Sanders's German military mission. On 2 August, the day after Germany declared war on Russia, Enver signed a secret Ottoman-German alliance; the arrival of the German battlecruiser Goeben and the light cruiser Breslau at Constantinople, ostensibly transferred to the Ottoman navy as Yavuz Sultan Selim and Midilli, cemented the alignment. On 29-30 October Yavuz and Midilli bombarded Russian Black Sea ports; Russia declared war on 2 November, Britain and France on 5 November. Sultan Mehmed V proclaimed jihad against the Entente on 14 November. In Egypt, formally still Ottoman but occupied by Britain since 1882, Britain declared a Protectorate on 18 December and deposed the pro-Ottoman khedive Abbas Hilmi II. In Persia the Ottoman-Russian frontier of Azerbaijan slid into war; the Great Arab Revolt in the Hijaz was still two years away.

Almost the whole continent, apart from Ethiopia and Liberia, was ruled from European capitals in 1914 under boundaries drawn at the 1884-85 Berlin Conference. Within days of the European declarations Allied troops from British and French African colonies invaded German Togoland (surrendered 26 August after the destruction of the Kamina wireless station), German Kamerun (a long campaign that would end in 1916), German South-West Africa (invaded from South Africa in September, though delayed by the Maritz Rebellion of Boer generals who opposed fighting Britain's war) and German East Africa, where Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck's askari force would tie down Allied units for the entire war. Prime Minister Louis Botha and Defence Minister Jan Smuts of the Union of South Africa, both former Boer generals, chose to support Britain and put down the Afrikaner rebellion by January 1915. In West Africa some 30,000 tirailleurs senegalais were being recruited under Blaise Diagne's advocacy for the French army. The Egyptian sultanate under Hussein Kamel was declared under British protectorate on 19 December. In Ethiopia the young Lij Iyasu succeeded Menelik II.

The Pacific dominions moved with startling speed after the outbreak. Within a month the Australian Naval and Military Expeditionary Force had seized German New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago at Bita Paka and Rabaul (11-17 September) at the cost of the first Australian combat deaths of the war; New Zealand troops occupied German Samoa on 29 August without a shot. Japan meanwhile took the German Marshall, Caroline and Mariana islands. Prime Minister Andrew Fisher's Labor government in Melbourne pledged Australia's last man and last shilling to Britain and raised the Australian Imperial Force under General William Bridges; more than 20,000 men sailed in a great convoy from Albany on 1 November, escorted by HMAS Sydney, which sank the German raider SMS Emden at Cocos Islands on 9 November. New Zealand's expeditionary force under Major General Alexander Godley sailed with them. Both would train in Egypt over the winter and land at Gallipoli on 25 April 1915. In Fiji, Tonga, the Solomons and across Melanesia, British colonial administrations recruited a much smaller stream of Pacific islander labour.

sources.title: The Outbreak of the First World War
